file_name: Dakhli and Barhoumi - 2023 - A skin lesion classification method based on expanding the surrounding lesion-shaped border for an e.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an end-to-end Inception-ResNet-v2 deep learning classifier with a soft attention block to classify skin lesions into different cancer types. The method involves expanding the lesion-shaped border and processing the input through the deep learning model to achieve classification results on the HAM10000 and PH2 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL PAPER' in the header. It presents a novel method for skin lesion classification involving expanding the lesion-shaped border for an Inception-ResNet-v2 classifier. The paper includes original experimental results, comparing the proposed method against state-of-the-art techniques on the HAM10000 and PH2 datasets, and details specific metrics such as precision rates of 95.6% and 97.26%.

title: A skin lesion classification method based on expanding the surrounding lesion-shaped border for an end-to-end Inception-ResNet-v2 classifier
brief_summary: This study proposes a skin lesion classification method that expands the surrounding lesion-shaped border to capture critical diagnostic information beyond the segmented lesion area. The approach utilizes an end-to-end Inception-ResNet-v2 classifier with a soft attention block to process these expanded borders for classification. The method was evaluated on the HAM10000 and PH2 datasets, employing data augmentation to address class imbalance. Experimental results demonstrated that expanding the border by 6 pixels yielded optimal performance, achieving precision rates of 95.6% on HAM10000 and 97.26% on PH2. These findings indicate that incorporating the lesion-shaped border significantly improves classification accuracy compared to using only the segmented lesion or bounding boxes.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions into specific cancer types. For the HAM10000 dataset, it classifies images into 7 classes: Nevoid nevus (NV), Basal cell carcinoma (BCC), Actinic keratosis intraepithelial carcinoma (AKIEC), Benign keratosis lesion (BKL), Dermatofibroma (DF), Vascular lesion (VASC), and Melanoma (MEL). For the PH2 dataset, it classifies images into 3 classes: Melanoma (MEL), Common Nevus (CN), and Atypical Nevus (AN). The model uses an end-to-end Inception-ResNet-v2 classifier with a soft attention block to assign each input image to one of these discrete diagnostic categories.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large publicly available dataset of dermoscopic images of skin lesions, including classes' labels and segmentation masks. It is known for having a huge imbalance in the number of images per class.

use.training: Used for offline training of the Inception-ResNet-v2 classifier. Data augmentation (random horizontal/vertical flips, translations, rotations) was applied to balance classes and avoid overfitting, resulting in 51,699 balanced images.
use.testing: Used for testing the classifier on 828 new images to evaluate precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.
use.other: Ground truth segmentation masks provided by the dataset were used to segment lesions before expanding the border.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermoscopic images used for skin lesion classification, containing segmentation masks.

use.training: Used for offline training of the Inception-ResNet-v2 classifier. Data augmentation was applied to balance classes, resulting in 15,535 balanced images.
use.testing: Used for testing the classifier on 30 new images to evaluate precision, recall, and F1-score.
use.other: Ground truth segmentation masks provided by the dataset were used to segment lesions before expanding the border.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To report the variability of performance metrics (accuracy, recall, precision, AUC, F1-score) across different expansion sizes and classes, indicating the effectiveness and stability of the classifier.
To analyze misclassification patterns between specific skin cancer classes (e.g., BCC vs MEL, BKL vs NV) and identify visually similar lesions.
To compare the proposed method's precision against baseline methods (bounding boxes and segmented lesions) to quantify performance improvements (e.g., 2.8% increase on HAM10000).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (precision, recall, F1-score, AUC) and standard deviation across different expansion sizes and classes.

conclusions: The proposed method, which expands the surrounding lesion-shaped border by 6 pixels to capture critical diagnostic information beyond the segmented lesion area, is effective for skin lesion classification.
The method achieved precision rates of 95.6% on the HAM10000 dataset and 97.26% on the PH2 dataset using an end-to-end Inception-ResNet-v2 classifier with a soft attention block.

Expanding the lesion-shaped border outperforms baseline methods that use only the segmented lesion, morphological dilation, or bounding boxes, increasing precision by 2.8% compared to bounding boxes and 4.8% compared to segmented lesions on the HAM10000 dataset.

The inclusion of the surrounding skin context in a lesion-shaped manner provides more informative features than using the whole dermoscopic image or only the segmented lesion, addressing limitations in segmentation and lesion similarity.

The dermatofibroma (DF) class achieved the highest performance with precision, recall, and F1-score of 100%, while melanoma (MEL) and benign keratosis (BKL) classes showed lower precision due to visual similarity with other classes.
The soft attention mechanism adjoined to the last fully connected layer of the Inception-ResNet-v2 model improves focus on the most important features, contributing to the model's effectiveness.
important_remarks: The study relies on ground truth segmentation masks provided by the HAM10000 and PH2 datasets for lesion segmentation, which may introduce bias if the masks are imperfect or not representative of real-world segmentation errors.

The method was evaluated exclusively on two public datasets (HAM10000 and PH2); lack of external validation on independent clinical datasets limits the assessment of generalizability to diverse populations or imaging devices.

Performance varies significantly by class; Melanoma (MEL) had the lowest precision (76%) on HAM10000, and Benign Keratosis (BKL) was frequently confused with Nevoid Nevus (NV), indicating challenges with visually similar lesions.

Data augmentation was used to balance classes, resulting in a training set of 51,699 images for HAM10000; while this addresses class imbalance, it may lead to overfitting on augmented patterns rather than true diagnostic features.

The expansion size (S=6 pixels) was determined empirically; the optimal border size may vary across different datasets or lesion types, limiting the method's robustness without dataset-specific tuning.

The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) to confirm the significance of performance improvements over baselines, relying instead on descriptive statistics and standard deviations.

Dermatofibroma (DF) achieved perfect scores (100% precision/recall/F1), which may indicate a lack of difficulty in the test set for this class or potential data leakage if similar samples exist in training.

The method processes only the expanded lesion-shaped border, discarding the rest of the dermoscopic image; this restricts the model's ability to utilize global context or perilesional features outside the expanded border.
